Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## AASEG: ATTENTION AWARE NETWORK FOR REAL TIME SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we present a new network named Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic image segmentation. Our network incorporates spatial and channel information using Spatial Attention (SA) and Channel Attention (CA) modules respectively. It also uses dense local multi-scale context information using Multi Scale Context (MSC) module. The feature maps are concatenated individually to produce the final segmentation map. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method using a comprehensive analysis, quantitative experimental results and ablation study using Cityscapes, ADE20K and Camvid datasets. Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Semantic Segmentation is a fundamental problem in computer vision where the goal is to label each and every pixel in the image to its appropriate class. Since it is required to be deployed in real world settings like robots and autonomous vehicles, hence there is a need to balance the speed vs performance tradeoff. Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) (Long et al., 2015) composed of convolutional layers was one of the first works to get strong semantic representation.

However, this method was not able to capture boundary information accurately. Atrous convolutions (Yu and Koltun, 2015) at the last several stages of their network was used to give feature maps with strong semantic representation, thus solving the problem with FCN based architectures. However, this comes at at the cost of increased computational complexity. For real world navigation tasks like autonomous driving, there is a need to improve the Frame Per Second(FPS).

Chen et al. (2017) and (Yu and Koltun, 2015) used dilated convolutions to increase the recpetive field while maintaining the number of parameters. SegNet (Badrinarayanan et al., 2017) utilizes a small network structure and the skip-connected method to achieve improved FPS. (Mehta et al., 2018), (Paszke et al., 2016) and (Poudel et al., 2019) proposed unique approaches to tackle real time semantic segmentation problem.

## 2 RELATED WORK

(Fu et al., 2019) introduces spatial-wise and channelwise attention modules to enhance the recpetive field (Paszke et al., 2016) trims a a lot of convolution filters to reduce computation. ICNet (Zhao et al., 2018a) proposed an image cascade network using multiresolution branches. (Iandola et al., 2016) allows the neural network to find the critical channels of the feature map and select the most suitable channels by itself.

ESPNet (Mehta et al., 2018) introduces an efficient spatial pyramid (ESP), which brings great improvement in both speed and performance. Bilateral Segmentation Network (BiSeNet) (Yu et al., 2018a) used two parts: Spatial Path (SP) is used to get with the loss of spatial information and Context Path (CP) for compressing the receptive field. (Yu et al., 2020) used multi-path framework to combine the low-level details and high-level semantics.

(Li et al., 2019b) utilizes a light-weight backbone to speed up its network and a multi scale feature aggregation to improve accuracy. SwiftNet (Orsic et al., 2019) used lateral connections to restore

We summarize our main contributions as follows:

• We propose a novel Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic segmentation.

• Our network is comprised of three parts: Spatial Attention(SA) module to capture the spatial dependencies of the feature maps, Channel Attention(CA) module to extract high level semantic information and Multi Scale Context(MSC) module to learn the information flow between feature maps of consecutive levels.

• Detailed experiments and analysis indicate the efficacy of our proposed network in not only improving the performance but also FPS. We achieve results on par with previous state of the art using Cityscapes, Camvid and on ADE20K datasets.

## 3 BACKGROUND

## 3.1 SPATIAL INFORMATION

The spatial information of the image is important to predict the detailed output for semantic segmentation. Modern existing approaches uses encoding of spatial information. DUC (Wang et al., 2018), PSPNet (Zhao et al., 2017), DeepLab v2 (Chen et al., 2017) use the dilated convolution to preserve the spatial size of the feature map.

## 3.2 CONTEXT INFORMATION

Semantic segmentation requires context information to generate a high-quality result. Most of the used methods enlarge the receptive field or fuse different contextual information. (Chen et al., 2017), (Wang et al., 2018) and (Yu and Koltun, 2015) use different dilation rates in convolution layers to capture different scale contextual information. In (Chen et al., 2017), an “ASPP” module is used to capture context information of different receptive field. PSPNet (Zhao et al., 2017) applies a “PSP” module which contains several different scales of average pooling layers.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 3.3 ATTENTION MECHANISM

(Hu et al., 2018) applied channel attention for image recognition and achieve the state-of-the-art. (Yu et al., 2018b) proposed a network that learns the global context as attention and revise the features. Multi scale network along with a custom attention module was proposed by (Sagar and Soundrapandiyan, 2020) for semantic segmentation.

## 3.4 FEATURE FUSION

The spatial information captured by the Spatial Path consists of rich detailed information. The output feature of the Context Path is made up of contextual information. Feature fusion was used to achieve state of the art results on image classification, object detection and instance segmentation using DMSANet (Sagar, 2021b).

## 4 PROPOSED METHOD

## 4.1 DATASET

The following datasets have been used to benchmark our results:

**1. Cityscapes** It is used for urban street segmentation. The 5000 annotated images are used in our experiments which are divided into 2975, 500 and 1525 images for training, validation, and testing respectively.
**2. ADE20K** This dataset contains labels of 150 object categories. The dataset includes 20k,2k and 3k images for training, validation and testing respectively.
**3. CamVid** This dataset is used for semantic segmentation for autonomous driving scenarios. It is composed of 701 densely annotated images.

## 4.2 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

The fundamental goal in semantic segmentation is to map an RGB image _X ∈ R[H][×][W][ ×]_[3] to a semantic map _Y ∈ R[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ with the same spatial resolution _H × W_ , where _C_ is the number of classes of objects present in image. The input image _X_ is converted to a set of feature maps _Fl_ where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map.

Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.

We express a convolution layer _W[n]_ ( _x_ ) as follows:

where _⊙_ represents the convolution operator, _W[n][×][n]_ represents the _n × n_ convolutional kernel, _x_ represents the input data and _b_ represents the bias vector.

## 4.3 MULTI SCALE CONTEXT MODULE

The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together. The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256. The feature map produced is of size _H × W × nc_ , where _H, W_ are the height and width of the feature map, and _nc_ denotes the number of channels.

The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions. At the final step, the outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature map.

## 4.8 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

PyTorch deep learning framework is used to carry out our experiments. Our neural networks is trained with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) as optimizer, batch size of 16, momentum of 0.9 and weight decay of 5 _e[−]_[4] . The network is trained for 20K iterations with an initial learning rate value of 0.01. The, “poly” learning rate policy is used to decay the initial learning rate while training the model to reduce over-fitting.
Data augmentation operations like random horizontal flip, random resizing with scale range of [1.0, 2.0], and random cropping with crop size of 1024 _×_ 1024 was done.

## 4.9 EVALUATION METRICS

For quantitative evaluation of the performance of our network, mean of class-wise intersection-overunion (mIoU) is used for bench-marking the performance, number of float-point operations (FLOPs) and frames per second (FPS) are used for bench-marking the speed.

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we present a new network named Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real time semantic segmentation. We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost. The fused spatial-channel attention along with Multi Scale Context(MSC) module enables network to extract discriminative and robust features of targets or background. We test our network using Cityscapes, ADE20K and Camvid datasets. Our network balances speed-performance tradeoff at par with previous state of the art network architectures. In the future, we would like to use multi scale attention module for instance segmentation problem.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real-time semantic segmentation, aiming to balance accuracy and inference speed through the integration of spatial and channel attention mechanisms, along with multi-scale context modeling. While the motivation is clear and aligned with the needs of real-time applications such as autonomous driving, the overall quality and clarity of the manuscript suffer from several critical issues that undermine its scientific rigor and reproducibility. Most notably, the paper abruptly cuts off mid-sentence in Section 4.3 (“MUL”), suggesting either a submission error or severe incompleteness—this is a fundamental flaw that precludes proper evaluation of the core technical contribution. Furthermore, the absence of a backbone network—a significant architectural choice—is mentioned but not sufficiently justified or analyzed, especially given that nearly all state-of-the-art methods rely on pretrained backbones for feature extraction; this omission raises concerns about fairness in comparison and scalability of the approach. While Figure 1 is referenced to support claims of superior speed-accuracy trade-offs, the figure itself is missing, limiting the ability to validate these assertions. The Related Work and Background sections contain several typographical errors (e.g., “recpetive,” “dosen’t”) and at times read as a disjointed list of citations rather than a cohesive synthesis of the field. Additionally, the prose throughout lacks precision—for instance, the claim that “feature maps are concatenated individually” is ambiguous and technically unclear. The abstract and introduction overstate performance gains (e.g., 74.4% mIoU on Cityscapes) without clarifying inference settings (e.g., input resolution, hardware) or training protocols, making comparisons to prior work questionable. In its current form, the paper fails to meet minimum standards for completeness and clarity, and substantial revisions—including restoring missing content, clarifying the architecture and training setup, providing complete experimental details, and correcting language issues—are necessary before it can be properly assessed.

comments.title: Incorrect and misleading cross-entropy loss definition for multi-class segmentation
comments.quote:
We use cross-entropy loss function to weigh the difference between the forward propagation result of the network and the ground truth of the samples. The cross-entropy loss is calculated as defined in Equation 8:

where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: The paper describes a binary cross-entropy loss using a single 'true' probability _yn_ and its complement 1-_yn_, which is fundamentally inconsistent with the multi-class nature of semantic segmentation (e.g., 19 classes in Cityscapes). While Equation 9 correctly uses a _K_-class formulation for the auxiliary loss, the description of Equation 8 misrepresents the training objective. The phrasing implies a binary outcome rather than per-pixel, multi-class classification, which could mislead readers about the actual loss used. The correct form should involve a summation over classes and comparison to one-hot encoded ground truth. This is not just informal phrasing—it constitutes a significant technical inaccuracy in a core component of the training pipeline, affecting reproducibility and method understanding.

comments.title: Major inconsistency in dilated convolution configurations between MSC module and ablation study
comments.quote: The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. ... We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.
comments.explanation: The paper states in Section 4.3 that the MSC module uses dilation rates 3, 6, and 12, a common setup for capturing multi-scale context. However, the ablation study (Table 6) evaluates configurations labeled 'r=1', 'r=2', 'r=3' and refers to a sequence '0,1,2,3', with no mention of 3,6,12. This creates a critical contradiction: it is unclear whether the final model uses large or small dilation rates. Furthermore, 'dilation rate 0' is invalid (dilation = 1 is standard), suggesting confusion in reporting. The ablation results (e.g., r=3 achieving 80.2% mIoU) appear superior to the main result (74.4%), yet the mapping between ablation settings and the final architecture is never clarified. This undermines confidence in the method description and experimental validity, representing a major methodological gap.

comments.title: Inconsistent mIoU values between main results and ablation study suggest benchmarking confusion
comments.quote:
|AASeg|74.8|74.4|202.7| in Table 1

|bilinear upsampling|**79.2**| in Table 4
comments.explanation: The main result in Table 1 reports 74.8% mIoU (val) for AASeg, consistent with 74.4% on the test set. However, the ablation study in Table 4 lists a variant (bilinear upsampling) achieving 79.2% on the same Cityscapes validation set—nearly 5 percentage points higher. Table 6 further shows a variant ('AASeg-(r=3)') reaching 80.2% mIoU, significantly exceeding the main result. The paper fails to clarify whether these are early prototypes, differently trained models, or fully equivalent variants. If such performance is achievable under the same setup, it should be reported as the main result or justified as non-default. The lack of clarity raises concerns about selective reporting and undermines the credibility of the benchmarking process.

comments.title: Ambiguity and underspecification in MSC module design and integration
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1×1 convolution, 3×3 convolution and 5×5 convolution respectively. ... The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.
comments.explanation: The description of the Multi Scale Context (MSC) module is internally inconsistent and underspecified. It first describes a multi-branch approach using 1×1, 3×3, and 5×5 convolutions, then shifts to a sequence of dilated convolutions with rates 3,6,12 and recursive concatenation—without clarifying how these components relate. Are they parallel paths? Is one a refinement of the other? The integration is not explained, and no equation or structural diagram in the text resolves this. Since MSC is a core contribution, the lack of a clear, unified description makes reproduction impossible and calls into question the novelty and coherence of the module design.

comments.title: Ambiguous and potentially inefficient feature aggregation mechanism in Equation 5
comments.quote: _xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ))
comments.explanation: Equation 5 applies three transformations (SA, CA, MSC) to the same input, performs element-wise multiplication with the input, and concatenates the results. This would triple the channel dimension without any mention of subsequent dimensionality reduction (e.g., 1×1 convolutions). While possible, this design choice significantly increases computational cost—problematic for a real-time model claiming 202.7 FPS. The text refers to 'features of the two branches' despite three terms, indicating possible drafting errors. Without clarification, it is unclear whether this is a parallel fusion mechanism or if the notation misrepresents the actual architecture. The lack of dimensionality discussion and contradictory phrasing renders the aggregation mechanism ambiguous and raises efficiency concerns.

comments.title: Implausible channel dimension (2048) in lightweight, backbone-free architecture
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1×1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The paper states that a feature map with 2048 channels is reduced to 256, which is highly suspicious in the context of a lightweight, backbone-free real-time segmentation model. AASeg claims efficiency (e.g., 0.76M parameters), yet intermediate feature maps of 2048 channels would involve massive computational load—especially when processed with dilated convolutions. Standard efficient designs (e.g., MobileNet, ESPNet) avoid such wide layers. This value appears inconsistent with the reported FPS and parameter count, suggesting either a typographical error (e.g., 512 or 1024 intended) or an unexplained architectural choice. The paper should justify or correct this value to maintain credibility.

comments.title: Unclear or missing definitions for core attention modules and loss functions
comments.quote:
The spatial attention module in our network is defined below:

where _W_ 1 and _W_ 2 denotes the first and second 1×1 convolution layer respectively...
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly claims that modules are 'defined below' but omits the actual equations—for both spatial attention, channel attention, and the class attention loss (Lcls). While figures may illustrate the flow, formal mathematical definitions are missing for central components. The 'class attention loss' is particularly problematic: it references a_{ijk} as 'class attention map', but standard channel attention (e.g., SE-block) produces per-channel weights, not per-pixel, per-class maps. Without a clear definition, it is impossible to determine whether this is a novel attention mechanism or a mislabeled auxiliary prediction. This lack of specification hinders reproducibility and critical evaluation of the method's contributions.

comments.title: Inconsistent feature map resolution notation suggesting hierarchical design where none exists
comments.quote: The input image _X_ is converted to a set of feature maps _Fl_ where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map.
comments.explanation: The use of subscripted spatial dimensions H_l and W_l implies that feature maps change resolution across stages, typical in encoder-decoder networks. However, the paper states that no backbone or spatial downscaling (e.g., strided convolutions) is used, suggesting that resolution is preserved throughout. This renders the subscript notation misleading—it implies a hierarchical, multi-scale architecture that is not present. The notation should be corrected to H and W (without subscripts) to reflect constant resolution, or the paper should clarify if and where down/up-sampling occurs. As written, it creates confusion about the network's architectural philosophy.

comments.title: Ambiguous naming and interpretation of ablation study variables
comments.quote:
We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.

|AASeg-(r=1)|79.8|...
comments.explanation: The ablation study uses labels like 'r=1', 'r=2', 'r=3', suggesting the maximum or sole dilation rate used. However, the text describes using a sequence '0,1,2,3', which could imply cumulative application. This conflict makes it unclear whether performance improvements come from adding larger rates incrementally or just using a single higher rate. Moreover, 'rate 0' is not valid in convolution operations. The mismatch between textual description and table labels renders the ablation study difficult to interpret and weakens the paper's ability to support architectural choices with evidence.

comments.title: Overstated or ambiguous performance claims in abstract and tables
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims AASeg 'performs better than most previous architectures', but Table 1 shows STDC2-Seg75 (76.8%) and STDC1-Seg75 (75.3%) achieve higher mIoU. While 'most' may statistically hold (>50%), it risks overstatement in a competitive benchmark. Additionally, Table 3 claims SOTA on ADE20K (46.29%), but no comparison is made to strong contemporaneous models (e.g., SegFormer), and FLOPs may not be computed under uniform conditions (e.g., input resolution not specified for ADE20K). These issues combine to make efficiency and accuracy claims appear stronger than fully justified, potentially misleading readers about the method's relative standing.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel Attention Aware Network (AASeg) for real-time semantic segmentation, aiming to balance accuracy and inference speed through the integration of spatial and channel attention mechanisms, along with multi-scale context modeling. While the motivation is clear and aligned with the needs of real-time applications such as autonomous driving, the overall quality and clarity of the manuscript suffer from several critical issues that undermine its scientific rigor and reproducibility. Most notably, the paper abruptly cuts off mid-sentence in Section 4.3 (“MUL”), suggesting either a submission error or severe incompleteness—this is a fundamental flaw that precludes proper evaluation of the core technical contribution. Furthermore, the absence of a backbone network—a significant architectural choice—is mentioned but not sufficiently justified or analyzed, especially given that nearly all state-of-the-art methods rely on pretrained backbones for feature extraction; this omission raises concerns about fairness in comparison and scalability of the approach. While Figure 1 is referenced to support claims of superior speed-accuracy trade-offs, the figure itself is missing, limiting the ability to validate these assertions. The Related Work and Background sections contain several typographical errors (e.g., “recpetive,” “dosen’t”) and at times read as a disjointed list of citations rather than a cohesive synthesis of the field. Additionally, the prose throughout lacks precision—for instance, the claim that “feature maps are concatenated individually” is ambiguous and technically unclear. The abstract and introduction overstate performance gains (e.g., 74.4% mIoU on Cityscapes) without clarifying inference settings (e.g., input resolution, hardware) or training protocols, making comparisons to prior work questionable. In its current form, the paper fails to meet minimum standards for completeness and clarity, and substantial revisions—including restoring missing content, clarifying the architecture and training setup, providing complete experimental details, and correcting language issues—are necessary before it can be properly assessed.

comments.title: Inconsistent feature map resolution notation in network architecture
comments.quote: The input image _X_ is converted to a set of feature maps _Fl_ where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears to imply that feature maps from different stages have potentially different spatial dimensions H_l and W_l, which is typical in encoder-style networks. However, the paper later claims the network does not use a backbone and processes the input through lightweight blocks, without mentioning any spatial downscaling (e.g., via strided convolutions or pooling). The absence of such operations would mean H_l = H and W_l = W across all stages. While this could be consistent with the authors' intent (a non-hierarchical design), the use of subscripted H_l and W_l suggests spatial resolution changes, which contradicts the overall architecture implied elsewhere. Unlike typical segmentation networks that downsample and upsample, AASeg seems to maintain full resolution throughout, so using H_l and W_l is misleading. This notation implies a multi-scale encoder-decoder structure that isn't present. The paper would be clearer if it noted whether spatial resolution is preserved or changed, and if preserved, used consistent H and W.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Multi Scale Context (MSC) module design
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 × 1 convolution, 3 × 3 convolution and 5 × 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together. ... The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions.
comments.explanation: There is confusion about how the MSC module is structured. First, it says the feature map is split and processed via different kernel sizes (1×1, 3×3, 5×5), then fused. But then it describes a dilated convolution path with rates 3, 6, 12 and recursive concatenation of outputs. The connection between these two descriptions is unclear. Are these two separate paths (multi-branch + dilated)? Is the dilated branch part of the multi-scale processing? The description reads like two different mechanisms are being described separately without integration. Figure 4 shows 'MSC' as a single block, but the text doesn't clarify how the kernel branches and the dilated convolutions relate. This makes reproduction difficult. Given that the final aggregation (Equation 5) applies fMSC to the same input as fSA and fCA, it’s likely that MSC is a single transformation, but its internal structure is underspecified. This is especially problematic since multi-scale context is a core contribution.

comments.title: Error in cross-entropy loss definition
comments.quote: where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: The paper defines a binary cross-entropy loss, but semantic segmentation is a multi-class (or pixel-wise multi-class) problem, not binary. The use of _yn_ and 1-_yn_ suggests two classes, but segmentation typically involves many classes (e.g., 19 for Cityscapes). The proper form should involve summing over classes, using log-likelihood for the true class. While binary cross-entropy can be used in a one-vs-all manner, the formulation here is misleading and incomplete. Equation 8 is missing in the context, but the prose description implies a flawed understanding of loss formulation. The auxiliary loss (Equation 9) correctly uses multi-class formulation with K classes. This suggests that the description of Equation 8 is an error in prose, not in actual implementation. However, stating that cross-entropy uses only 'true' and 'false' probabilities is incorrect and could mislead readers about the method.

comments.title: Missing kernel size in convolution notation
comments.quote: We express a convolution layer _W[n]_ ( _x_ ) as follows: where _⊙_ represents the convolution operator, _W[n][×][n]_ represents the _n × n_ convolutional kernel, _x_ represents the input data and _b_ represents the bias vector.
comments.explanation: The text defines W[n](x) but doesn’t provide the actual equation. While this might be due to OCR or passage truncation, within the provided context, it's a gap. The explanation defines the symbols but doesn't show how they are used in a formula (e.g., W[n](x) = W[n]×n ⊙ x + b). Given that this is presented as a mathematical definition, the absence of the expression is problematic. However, since the passage is cut off (the next section starts immediately), this may be a boundary artifact rather than a real error in the full paper. Therefore, it's likely not a flaw in the original work, just in the extracted text.

comments.title: Contradiction in class attention loss definition
comments.quote: The class attention loss _Lcls_ from channel attention module is also used. The class attention loss is where _aijk_ is the value generated of the class attention map of the _j[th]_ pixel in the _i[th]_ sample for the _k[th]_ class.
comments.explanation: The loss function is mentioned but not provided — the equation is missing. More importantly, the term 'class attention loss' is introduced, but no definition is given. Is this a regularization loss? A consistency loss? The auxiliary loss (Laux) is properly defined over predictions and ground truth, but Lcls involves 'aijk', which is outputs of the channel attention module. However, channel attention typically produces channel weights (C values per feature map), not per-pixel class attention maps. Using 'class attention' suggests per-class spatial maps, but CA modules like SE-blocks output 1D channel weights. This raises a conflict: either the CA module is redefined as producing spatial-class maps (unusual), or 'class attention loss' refers to something else (e.g., auxiliary prediction from attention path). The paper lacks clarity on this loss's form and purpose, making it impossible to assess its contribution from the text.

comments.title: Ambiguity in feature concatenation in aggregation equation
comments.quote: _xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ))
comments.explanation: The equation applies three transformations (SA, CA, MSC) to the same input _xconcat_, then element-wise multiplies each with _xconcat_ using _⊗_, and finally concatenates them with _⊕_. This is unusual: typically, attention modules apply multiplicative gating (e.g., SA(x) ⊗ x), but concatenating the outputs of three such gated features would drastically increase channel count. The output dimensions are not discussed. Moreover, if SA, CA, and MSC all transform the same input, and the results are concatenated, the final tensor has 3× the original channels — but no channel reduction is mentioned. This could lead to high computational cost, contradicting the real-time claim. The paper should clarify whether there is a 1×1 conv after to reduce dimensionality. While not necessarily an error, the omission makes the method underspecified and raises concerns about scalability and efficiency.

comments.title: Overstatement of performance on ADE20K
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: The abstract states the network 'performs better than most previous architectures', but Table 1 shows that STDC2-Seg75 achieves 76.8% mIoU (higher than AASeg's 74.4%) at lower FPS, and STDC1-Seg75 also achieves 75.3%. So AASeg is not superior in accuracy to all but a few — it is outperformed by at least two. The claim 'better than most' is statistically plausible (if 'most' means >50%), but in context, where higher-performing models exist, it risks overstating. More importantly, in Table 3 (ADE20K), AASeg achieves 46.29% mIoU, labeled as best, but the reference 'DCANet' achieves 45.49% with unknown FLOPs (marked '-'). However, SFNet is listed twice with different mIoU (42.81 and 44.67), suggesting different variants. The paper doesn't compare to more recent or higher-performing models (e.g., SegFormer, MaskFormer), but since it’s under review for ICLR 2022, that’s acceptable if the comparison is limited to cited works. The claim in the abstract is specific to Cityscapes, so the issue is only with 'most', which is vague but not false.

comments.title: Incorrect cross-entropy loss definition for multi-class semantic segmentation
comments.quote:
We use cross-entropy loss function to weigh the difference between the forward propagation result of the network and the ground truth of the samples. The cross-entropy loss is calculated as defined in Equation 8:

where _N_ denotes the total number of samples, _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- _yn_ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: The description of the cross-entropy loss suggests a binary formulation (with a single 'true' probability _yn_ and its complement 1-_yn_), which is inconsistent with the context of multi-class semantic segmentation. In such tasks, the standard cross-entropy loss operates on per-pixel class probabilities over C classes, not a single scalar 'true' likelihood. The paper mentions _K_ categories in the auxiliary loss (Equation 9), confirming a multi-class setting. Yet no such structure is evident in the undefined Equation 8. While the text might be attempting a high-level summary, the phrasing misrepresents the loss function's form — especially the use of 'the probability that the forward propagation result is true' — which implies label-smoothing or a binary outcome, not per-class logits. This is not merely informal: it contradicts standard usage and could mislead readers about the training objective. The correct multi-class cross-entropy should involve a summation over classes and use one-hot encoded ground truth. No such form is even alluded to, despite being fundamental. Given that the loss directly affects training and reproducibility, the omission is significant.

comments.title: Ambiguous and inconsistent use of concatenation operator in Equation 5
comments.quote:
_xAASeg_ = (( _fSA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fCA_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ ) _⊕_ ( _fMSC_ ( _xconcat_ ) _⊗ xconcat_ )) (5)

where _⊕_ represents the concatenation operator, _fCA_ represents the channel attention module mentioned in Equation 2, _fSA_ represents the spatial attention module, _fMSC_ represents the multi scale attention and _xconcat_ represents the combined feature.
comments.explanation: The equation shows three terms being concatenated via _⊕_, but each term is a Hadamard product (implied by _⊗_) between an attention map and the input _xconcat_. If _fSA_, _fCA_, and _fMSC_ all output attention maps of the same spatial size as _xconcat_, then the element-wise multiplication results in feature maps of the same dimension. Concatenating these three would triple the channel dimension, which may be intended. However, the preceding definition of _⊕_ refers to _x_1, _x_2, and _x_3 as 'features of the two branches' — a clear contradiction since there are three terms. The phrase 'two branches' is likely an OCR or drafting error, but even with correction to 'three', the module design is unclear: are SA, CA, and MSC applied in parallel and then concatenated? That would differ from typical attention mechanisms, which usually reweight the input and sum or replace it. Concatenating reweighted versions would preserve all information but increase channel count significantly, requiring a subsequent 1×1 conv to reduce dimension — which is not mentioned. Given that the network claims efficiency (202.7 FPS), a tripling of feature maps without explanation raises concerns. While Figure 4 may clarify, the equation and text alone are underspecified and ambiguous about the actual fusion mechanism.

comments.title: Inconsistency in number of channels (2048 vs. later results)
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 _×_ 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The Multi Scale Context module states a reduction from 2048 to 256 channels. While such a dimension may be plausible in deep networks, AASeg claims to be a lightweight, backbone-free design optimized for real-time performance. A 2048-channel intermediate layer would imply very high computational cost — especially if processed through dilated convolutions with large kernels — which appears inconsistent with the reported 202.7 FPS and low parameter count (e.g., AASeg-baseline at 0.76M parameters in Table 6). Standard lightweight models like MobileNet or ESPNet avoid such wide layers. Given that no other section mentions 2048 channels, and the final mIoU is achieved with no excessive FLOPs, it's possible that '2048' is an OCR error (e.g., intended as 512 or 1024). Alternatively, if accurate, the authors should justify how such a high-dimensional feature is maintained under stringent efficiency constraints. As it stands, the value raises a red flag about plausibility or notation consistency.

comments.title: Missing equations and definitions reduce reproducibility
comments.quote:
The spatial attention module in our network is defined below:

where _W_ 1 and _W_ 2 denotes the first and second 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer respectively...
comments.explanation: The text repeatedly states that a module is 'defined below' but then omits the actual mathematical definition or equation. This occurs for both the Spatial Attention (Section 4.4) and Channel Attention (Section 4.5) modules, as well as for Equation 7 referenced in Section 4.6. While Figure 2 and Figure 3 may depict these modules, a rigorous academic paper should provide formal expressions. For example, a standard spatial attention module might involve pooling followed by convolutions and a sigmoid gate, but the lack of an explicit formula prevents verification of novelty or correct implementation. Similarly, the claim that the channel attention 'extracts high-level multi-scale semantic information' is not clearly supported without knowing the structure (e.g., does it use dilated convolutions or multi-branch paths?). Given that attention modules are central to the contribution, their underspecification hinders reproducibility and evaluation.

comments.title: Inconsistent fusion description between abstract and method
comments.quote: The feature maps are concatenated individually to produce the final segmentation map. | Feature maps from three stages are concatenated individually to produce the final segmentation map.
comments.explanation: The abstract and summary claim that feature maps from three stages are concatenated to produce the final segmentation map. However, Section 4.6 describes an aggregation mechanism that combines attention-weighted paths (SA, CA, MSC) via concatenation, not stage-wise feature maps. Later, upsampling and fusion are mentioned in Equation 7, which is undefined. There is no clear description of how or when multi-stage features are fused. This contradicts the abstract's claim and raises concern about whether the final prediction actually involves concatenation of stage outputs — a detail critical for understanding the architecture's design. While 'feature maps from three stages' might refer to outputs of SA, CA, MSC within one stage, that conflicts with the earlier definition where _F_l refers to stage-wise network outputs (l=1,2,3). Without clarification, a reader may incorrectly assume skip connections from multiple stages are concatenated, which is a common design, but not evidently implemented here.

comments.title: Ambiguity in dilation rate sequence and its ablation
comments.quote: We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3. It seems large dilation rates increases the performance but comes at the cost of increased number of parameters, hence decrease in FPS. This effect is shown in Table 6:
comments.explanation: The text states the network uses dilation rates 0, 1, 2, 3. However, '0' is not a valid dilation rate (dilation 1 corresponds to standard convolution). This is likely a typo, with '0' meaning no dilation (i.e., rate 1) or being a placeholder. More concerning is that Table 6 compares 'AASeg-(r=1)', '(r=2)', '(r=3)', but no condition labeled 'r=0' exists. The 'AASeg-baseline' (79.2 mIoU) is listed separately and has higher FPS than the r=1 variant, suggesting it may be the 'no dilation' case. But the text says 'AASeg-w/o dilation' achieves only 77.4 mIoU, which contradicts the baseline. This suggests that the 'baseline' already includes some form of dilation or attention. The naming and alignment between text and table are deeply confusing: is r=1 using rates [1], [1,2], or [3,6,12] as in Section 4.3? The ablation study is valuable, but the mapping between the configuration labels and architectural choices is not defined, making it impossible to interpret the trade-offs.

comments.title: Inconsistent mIoU values between main results and ablation study
comments.quote:
|AASeg|74.8|74.4|202.7| in Table 1

|bilinear upsampling|**79.2**| in Table 4
comments.explanation: The main result in Table 1 reports a 74.8% mIoU on the Cityscapes validation set for AASeg, which aligns with the test set performance of 74.4%. However, the ablation study in Table 4 reports a mIoU of 79.2% using bilinear upsampling—nearly 5 percentage points higher—on the same Cityscapes validation set. This discrepancy is concerning because the ablation study is meant to evaluate variants of the final model, not a superior version. If 79.2% is achievable, it should be the main reported score unless that configuration differs significantly (e.g., longer training, different resolution). However, Table 6 shows AASeg-(r=3) achieves 80.2% mIoU, which is even higher. The paper does not clarify whether these ablation experiments are full models or early prototypes. Given that the ablation study uses the 'Cityscapes validation set' without qualification and presents higher performance than the main result, it creates confusion about which configuration is the final one and whether the main result underreports performance. This undermines confidence in the reported benchmarking and may mislead readers about the model’s actual capability.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Table 4 header and column labeling
comments.quote:
|old.||
|---|---|
|Method|mIoU (%)|
comments.explanation: Table 4 begins with '|old.||' followed by a separator, suggesting OCR corruption. The intended header was likely '|Method|mIoU (%)|' as the columns appear. While OCR noise is expected, the persistence of 'old.'—which doesn't correspond to any defined term—is problematic. Even if it's an artifact, its presence raises questions: Is this an older version of the table? Was data merged incorrectly? Given that the table presents ablation results, clarity is essential. The structure is otherwise interpretable, but this artifact introduces unnecessary ambiguity in a formal academic context. Since the column headers appear correct immediately after, the meaning can be inferred, but it remains a presentation flaw that could mislead.

comments.title: Mismatch between text description and ablation table for dilation rates
comments.quote:
We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.

|AASeg-(r=1)|79.8|...
comments.explanation: The text states the dilation rates used are '0, 1, 2, 3', which suggests a sequence of experiments where each rate is applied individually. However, Table 6 uses labels like 'AASeg-(r=1)', 'AASeg-(r=2)', and 'AASeg-(r=3)', implying the maximum dilation rate. The term 'r=1' does not correspond to using rate 1 alone if '0,1,2,3' is the full set. If the ablation tests progressively adding larger dilation rates, then 'r=3' should represent using rates 1, 2, and 3 (or increasing the maximum), not just rate 3. Furthermore, 'AASeg-w/o dilation' presumably removes all dilated convolutions, but it is unclear whether 'r=1' uses only rate 1 or includes rate 1 along with smaller default rates. The naming suggests a single rate, conflicting with the prose description of a sequence. Without clarification, it is impossible to determine whether performance improves due to cumulative use of multi-rate context or just higher max rates. This ambiguity undermines the interpretability of Table 6’s conclusion about trade-offs.

comments.title: Contradiction in computational cost claims for kernel ablation
comments.quote:
Larger kernel size of 7×7 is also tried which results in a similar performance but increases computational complexity.

|k = 7|**79.5**|136.1| in Table 5
comments.explanation: The text correctly notes that larger kernels increase computational cost, which is supported by Table 5: GFLOPs rise from 118.2 (k=1) to 136.1 (k=7). However, it claims the performance is 'similar' while Table 5 shows k=5 and k=7 both achieve 79.5% (best), and k=1 achieves only 79.2%, a non-trivial 0.3% difference. In semantic segmentation, even 0.1% mIoU differences are often deemed significant. Saying the performance is 'similar' downplays a measurable gain. More importantly, if k=7 gives the best score, why is it not used in the main model? The paper does not justify the choice of kernel size in the final architecture, especially since GFLOPs were already reported for k=7 and the trend shows increasing cost with marginal gain. This lack of architectural justification constitutes an underspecification of method choices.

comments.title: GFLOPs in Table 3 may not be directly comparable with prior work
comments.explanation: AASeg achieves 46.29% mIoU with 80.26 GFLOPs on ADE20K, outperforming other models in both accuracy and efficiency (e.g., PSPNet101 at 238.4 GFLOPs). However, different models may use different input resolutions, batch sizes, or evaluation scripts to compute FLOPs. The paper does not specify the input resolution used for ADE20K FLOPs computation, while Cityscapes uses 512×1024. Without this, fair comparison is questionable. Moreover, some works report FLOPs only for the encoder or up to a certain stage. While Table 3 reports GFLOPs for AASeg, it does not confirm whether the other models (e.g., SFNet, DCANet) are evaluated under identical conditions. This makes the efficiency claim potentially misleading, though not necessarily erroneous. The lack of standardization details is a common but important gap in comparative analysis.

comments.title: Underspecified dilated convolution configuration in MSC module
comments.quote:
The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12.

...outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature map.
comments.explanation: Section 4.3 describes using dilation rates 3, 6, and 12 in the MSC module, but the ablation study in Table 6 mentions rates 0, 1, 2, 3. This is inconsistent: are the main model rates (3,6,12) or (1,2,3)? The ablation study suggests smaller rates are explored, but the main architecture claims higher ones. The text gives no indication that these are different components or different stages. If rates 3,6,12 are used in MSC, then the ablation on 'r=1' to 'r=3' must refer to a different module or a simplified version. But no such distinction is made. This contradiction leaves the actual architecture underspecified. Is the final model using (3,6,12) or one of the ablated settings? The main result (74.4% mIoU) should map to one configuration, but the link is missing. This is a major methodological gap.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The provided excerpt presents a clean, well-motivated study on real-time semantic segmentation, but it currently lacks the technical depth and critical synthesis required for a high-impact conference submission. While the stated performance metrics (74.4% mIoU at 202.7 FPS) are impressive, the introductory and related work sections rely heavily on a generic "lit review" format that lists prior methods without adequately diagnosing the specific limitations or failures in current state-of-the-art designs that your "AASeg" architecture specifically addresses. Furthermore, the manuscript suffers from inconsistent formatting and minor grammatical errors (e.g., redundant words, spacing issues around citations, and incomplete sentences in section 2) that detract from the professional quality expected at an ICLR-level venue. To strengthen the paper, the authors should move beyond a simple description of their components—Spatial, Channel, and Multi-Scale modules—and instead provide a more rigorous, comparative conceptual justification for why this specific combination of attention mechanisms overcomes the latency-accuracy trade-offs that have stymied previous lightweight architectures.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the definition of the AASeg module
comments.quote: xAASeg = (( fSA( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat ) ⊕ ( fCA( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat ) ⊕ ( fMSC( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat ))
comments.explanation: Equation (5) uses the symbol '⊕', which the authors define as concatenation, to perform what is functionally presented as an additive aggregation. This creates a technical contradiction between the stated operation and the implied mathematical result.

comments.title: Ambiguity and notational overlap in loss functions
comments.quote: where N denotes the total number of samples... where B is the mini batch size, N is the number of pixels in every batch
comments.explanation: Equation (8) describes a binary cross-entropy formulation that is mathematically insufficient for the multi-class semantic segmentation task specified in Section 4.2. Additionally, the variable 'N' is inconsistently defined across paragraphs 40 and 42, creating confusion regarding the scope of the loss computation.

comments.title: Architecture design and channel count contradiction
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 × 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the network does not use a standard backbone, yet the input to the MSC module (2048 channels) is characteristic of ResNet-101. This suggests a reliance on a heavy backbone that the authors explicitly claim to avoid, undermining the paper's primary architectural motivation.

comments.title: Statistical inconsistencies in ablation and performance reporting
comments.explanation: The ablation study (Table 6) reports performance (79.2% mIoU) significantly higher than the reported test set results (74.4% mIoU on Cityscapes), suggesting an inconsistency in training configurations. Furthermore, the framing of 'best' results across different tables and datasets lacks adequate context, making it difficult to verify the model's actual state-of-the-art standing.

While the stated performance metrics (74.4% mIoU at 202.7 FPS) are impressive, the introductory and related work sections rely heavily on a generic "lit review" format that lists prior methods without adequately diagnosing the specific limitations or failures in current state-of-the-art designs that your "AASeg" architecture specifically addresses. Furthermore, the manuscript suffers from inconsistent formatting and minor grammatical errors (e.g., redundant words, spacing issues around citations, and incomplete sentences in section 2) that detract from the professional quality expected at an ICLR-level venue. comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the definition of the AASeg module
comments.quote: xAASeg = (( fSA( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat ) ⊕ ( fCA( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat ) ⊕ ( fMSC( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat ))
comments.explanation: Equation (5) defines the aggregation as a summation of three terms, where the '⊕' symbol is explicitly defined as the concatenation operator. Concatenation of feature maps (which typically changes channel dimensions) is fundamentally different from summation (element-wise addition, ⊗ is often used for element-wise multiplication). If the authors intend for the branches to be concatenated, the result would have 3x the channels of a single original branch; if they intend for them to be summed, '⊕' is the wrong operator. Given the context of aggregation in segmentation networks, summation is common, but the use of '⊕' (for concatenation) creates a technical contradiction.

comments.title: Ambiguity in loss function definitions
comments.quote: where N denotes the total number of samples, yn denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and 1- yn denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation: Equation (8) is described in a way that suggests a binary classification cross-entropy, yet semantic segmentation with C classes (as defined in Section 4.2) requires a multi-class cross-entropy loss (typically categorical cross-entropy). comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in auxiliary loss definition

## High-Level Feedback

The paper presents AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network, but suffers from several significant issues that undermine its quality and clarity. Most critically, the paper appears to be incomplete or poorly compiled—Section 4.3 cuts off abruptly mid-sentence with "MUL," suggesting missing content or formatting errors that would disqualify it from conference review. The writing quality is substandard, with numerous grammatical errors ("dosen't," "recpetive field," "at at the cost"), awkward phrasing, and inconsistent citation formatting that create a poor first impression and raise concerns about scholarly rigor. The related work section reads as a disjointed list of prior methods without meaningful synthesis or clear positioning of the proposed approach within the literature. The contributions, while listed, are not clearly differentiated from existing attention-based segmentation methods, and the claimed novelty of combining spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context is questionable given the abundance of similar work. Methodological details are severely lacking—there is no description of the network architecture's depth, number of parameters, specific attention mechanisms used, or how the MSC module operates. The ablation study is mentioned but not presented, and the reported results of 74.4% mIOU at 202.7 FPS on Cityscapes lack comparison baselines with identical experimental conditions. The paper would require substantial rewriting, completing missing sections, improving language quality, and providing rigorous experimental details and comparisons before being considered for publication.

comments.title: Missing loss equation and undefined variables in section 4.7
comments.quote: where B is the mini batch size, N is the number of pixels in every batch; K is the number of categories; pijk is the prediction of the jth pixel in the ith sample for the kth class, I(gij = k) is a function which is defined in Equation 10.
comments.explanation: Equation 10 is not provided in the extracted text, so the definition of I(gij = k) is missing. The auxiliary loss Laux is defined in Equation 9, but the actual expression for Laux is not shown; only the variable definitions are given. Similarly, the class attention loss Lcls is described with variables aijk, but its equation (presumably Equation 11) is also missing. This makes it impossible to verify the correctness of the loss formulation or reproduce the training procedure.

comments.title: Ambiguity in network architecture description
comments.quote: Our network dosen't use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures. The input image is first passed through a block comprising of convolutional, batch normalization and ReLU activation function.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the network does not use any backbone, but they do not specify the output channels or number of layers in the initial convolutional block. Later, the Multi Scale Context module is said to reduce channels from 2048 to 256, implying that the initial feature map after that block has 2048 channels—a very large number for a lightweight real-time network. The architecture (Figure 4) is not shown in the text, so it's unclear how this is achieved without a backbone. This underspecification makes it difficult to reproduce the network or understand its parameter count, especially given the claim of 0.76M parameters in Table 6, which seems implausibly low for a network producing 2048-channel feature maps.

comments.title: Dilation rate sequence in ablation study lacks definition
comments.quote: AASeg-(r=1), AASeg-(r=2), AASeg-(r=3)
comments.explanation: It is unclear what 'r=1', 'r=2', 'r=3' refer to in Table 6. The text says 'We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.' but this is ambiguous: does it mean the three dilated convolutions in the MSC module use dilation rates (1,2,3), (2,3,4), or something else? Earlier in Section 4.3, the dilation rates are given as 3, 6, and 12. The ablation study uses different values, but the mapping from the notation 'r=x' to the actual dilation rates is not explained. This lack of specification makes the ablation non-reproducible.

comments.title: Underspecified aggregation operation
comments.quote: x_AASeg = ((fSA(x_concat) ⊗ x_concat) ⊕ (fCA(x_concat) ⊗ x_concat) ⊕ (fMSC(x_concat) ⊗ x_concat))
comments.explanation: Equation 5 defines the concatenation operator ⊕ and the element-wise multiplication ⊗, but it is unclear what operation ⊗ represents—presumably element-wise multiplication after applying attention weights. However, the dimension of the attention maps and the feature maps must match, and the text does not specify whether the attention maps are broadcast or reshaped. Additionally, the variable x_concat is not defined earlier; it appears to be the output of a concatenation of low-level and high-level features, but this is not explicitly stated. This lack of clarity could lead to implementation errors.

comments.title: Inconsistency in validation mIoU between main results (Table 1) and ablation study (Tables 4–6)
comments.quote:
Table 1: AASeg | 512_×_1024 | no | 74.8 | 74.4 | 202.7

comments.explanation: The main result in Table 1 reports a validation mIoU of 74.8% for AASeg on Cityscapes. The ablation is explicitly stated to be on the Cityscapes validation set, and the resolution (512×1024) matches. The discrepancy of roughly 4.4 percentage points is too large to be explained by minor architectural variations (e.g., upsampling method or kernel size) and undermines the reliability of the reported results. The authors do not clarify whether the ablation uses a different dataset split, training procedure, or evaluation protocol.

comments.title: Inconsistency in FPS between main result (Table 1) and ablation study (Table 6)
comments.explanation: The main results claim 202.7 FPS for AASeg at 512×1024 resolution, yet the ablation study reports only 118.2 FPS for the AASeg-baseline under the same resolution. No hardware or inference details are given for either measurement, so a reader cannot determine which is correct. The factor-of-1.7 difference suggests either different GPUs, batch sizes, or optimization settings were used, but the paper does not reconcile this. This makes the speed advantage claimed in the abstract and conclusion questionable.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of the multi-scale context module structure
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1 _×_ 1 convolution, 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 5 _×_ 5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together.
comments.explanation: The phrase “split to three different parts” is ambiguous: it could mean the input feature map is divided into three channel groups, or that three parallel convolutions are applied to the entire input. The subsequent sentence “The output fused feature map is first convolved…” implies the outputs of the three branches are concatenated (fused), but the number of output channels after each branch is not given. Moreover, later the module applies dilated convolutions on “the input feature map” — it is unclear whether this input is the fused result or the original feature map before the split. The flow is not clearly defined, hindering reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing justification for FLOPs and parameter counts in ablation tables
comments.quote: Table 6: ... FPS are estimated for an input image of resolution of 512 _×_ 1024. Best results are highlighted in bold.
comments.explanation: The paper presents FLOPs and FPS values but does not specify the hardware platform, software version, or batch size used for FPS measurement (except resolution). For a real-time segmentation paper, FPS is a critical metric; omitting hardware details makes the speed comparisons non-reproducible. Additionally, FLOPs are reported without clarifying whether they are multiply-adds or total operations, which is a common ambiguity in the field. This underspecification could mislead readers who attempt to compare with other methods.

comments.explanation: In Table 1, several methods achieve higher or comparable mIoU: STDC2-Seg75 reaches 76.8% test mIoU, BiSeNetV1 with ResNet18 gets 74.7%, SFNet gets 74.5%. AASeg's 74.4% is not clearly better than most; it is in the middle. Also, in terms of FPS, STDC1-Seg50 achieves 250.4 FPS, which is faster. The claim “better than most” is a framing exaggeration. The immediately following sentence defines symbols (⊙, W[n×n], x, b) that clearly belong to a standard convolution operation, confirming the authors intended to provide the mathematical forward-pass definition. This omission leaves the core computational step of the architecture mathematically undefined, which is critical for methodological clarity and reproducibility.

comments.title: Conceptual Inaccuracy Regarding Attention and Receptive Field
comments.quote: (Fu et al., 2019) introduces spatial-wise and channelwise attention modules to enhance the recpetive field
comments.explanation: I initially flagged the phrase 'enhance the recpetive field' because attention mechanisms do not alter or increase the receptive field; they re-weight feature responses spatially or channel-wise. The receptive field is strictly determined by kernel size, stride, and dilation rates. The authors likely confused 'receptive field' with 'semantic representation' or 'feature discrimination'. As written, this mischaracterizes a fundamental property of attention modules in the related work section.

comments.title: Contradictory Description of BiSeNet Spatial Path
comments.quote: Bilateral Segmentation Network (BiSeNet) (Yu et al., 2018a) used two parts: Spatial Path (SP) is used to get with the loss of spatial information and Context Path (CP) for compressing the receptive field.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'get with the loss of spatial information' is grammatically broken and logically contradictory to the actual BiSeNet architecture. The Spatial Path in BiSeNet is specifically designed to *preserve* high-resolution spatial details (avoiding the loss of spatial information) while the Context Path handles semantics. It is highly probable this is a transcription error for 'get without the loss' or 'retain spatial information'. As currently written, it incorrectly misrepresents a key baseline method's design philosophy.

comments.title: Inconsistent and Hard-to-Parse Notation for Feature Maps
comments.quote: The input image _X_ is converted to a set of feature maps _Fl_ where l=1,...,3 from each network stage, where _Fl ∈ R[H][l][W][l][C][l]_ is a _Cl_ -dimensional feature map.
comments.explanation: While the authors' intent to index feature maps by network level (l=1,2,3) is clear from context, the notation `_Fl` and `_Cl` lacks proper subscript formatting (likely an OCR/typesetting artifact resulting in `R[H][l][W][l][C][l]`). Standard mathematical convention would use $F_l$ and $C_l$. Although the meaning is recoverable, the current formatting violates standard notation consistency and reduces readability, especially when combined with the bracket-heavy dimension notation.

comments.title: Unexplained mIoU discrepancy between final results and ablation baselines
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports a validation mIoU of 74.8% for the final AASeg model on Cityscapes at 512×1024 resolution. However, the ablation studies in Tables 4, 5, and 6 report baseline validation mIoUs of 79.2% to 79.5% under what appears to be the identical dataset and resolution settings. A ~4.4-4.7% gap on Cityscapes is substantial and cannot be explained by the text. It is unclear whether different training iterations, learning rate schedules, or data augmentation settings were applied. This discrepancy casts doubt on the internal consistency of the ablation study, the reproducibility of the final reported metrics, and the validity of comparative baselines.

comments.title: Unjustified channel dimension in MSC module
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 × 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: Section 4.2 explicitly states: 'Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures.' In standard deep learning practice, reaching 2048 feature channels typically requires a deep residual backbone (e.g., ResNet-50). A single initial block of conv/bn/relu would not naturally produce 2048 channels. The specific mention of reducing from 2048 suggests the authors may have copy-pasted the channel reduction configuration from an architecture that does use a backbone, without adjusting it to their lightweight design. This lacks justification and confuses the network's actual capacity.

comments.title: Contradictory equation reference in loss definition
comments.quote: I ( gij = k ) is a function which is defined in Equation 10.
comments.explanation: The text states that the indicator function $I(g_{ij} = k)$ is defined in Equation 10. However, Equation 10 is the mathematical formulation of the auxiliary supervision loss ($L_{aux}$) itself. An indicator function is a standard, universally understood mathematical construct in this context and should not point to the loss equation. This appears to be a clear referencing error that misdirects the reader.

comments.title: Variable-to-branch mismatch in Aggregation section
comments.quote: where ⊕ represents the concatenation operator and x1, x2 and x3 represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The authors list three feature variables ($x_1, x_2, x_3$) but explicitly refer to 'the two branches'. Given Equation (5) concatenates three terms corresponding to the SA, CA, and MSC modules, the text likely contains a typo and intended to say 'three branches'. While easily fixable, it creates momentary confusion when mapping variables to architectural components.

comments.title: Ambiguous flow description in Multi Scale Context module
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 × 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256. ... The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions.
comments.explanation: The description of the MSC module shifts abruptly from a fused 256-channel map back to 'The input feature map'. It is unclear whether the dilated convolutions operate on the original feature map (before the 1×1 reduction) or the newly reduced 256-channel map. Additionally, the phrase 'at every stage' and the dense concatenation strategy for the dilated branches are described too vaguely for exact reproduction. Clarifying the data flow and specifying whether this is a parallel ASPP-like structure or a sequential/dense one would be necessary.

comments.title: Contradictory dilation rates between method description and ablation study
comments.quote: Section 4.3: "The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12." / Section 6: "We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3."
comments.explanation: Initially, the MSC module description in Section 4.3 clearly specifies that the network uses dilation rates of 3, 6, and 12 to capture multi-scale context. However, when reading the ablation study in Section 6, the authors abruptly claim to use a sequence of 0, 1, 2, 3, and Table 6 introduces model variants labeled `(r=1)`, `(r=2)`, `(r=3)`. I checked the surrounding text to see if `(r=k)` referred to a multiplier, a base rate replacement, or a different module, but the direct numerical contradiction between the proposed architecture and the ablation setup remains unresolved. This inconsistency makes it impossible to verify which dilation configuration actually produces the final results or to accurately reproduce the reported performance trade-offs. This is a major technical oversight.

comments.title: Ambiguous SFNet entries in ADE20K comparison table
comments.explanation: The authors provide a useful performance comparison on the ADE20K dataset in Table 3, which helps contextualize the method's accuracy. However, two rows list "SFNet" with differing Mean IoU and compute values but omit the backbone, resolution, and training settings for either entry. Unlike Tables 1 and 2, which systematically include these columns, this table lacks them. Without specifying which SFNet variant is being used, it is unclear whether the comparison is fair or how the computational costs align with the accuracy gains. This omission could mislead readers assessing the efficiency-accuracy trade-off.

comments.title: Unclear computational metric label in Table 5
comments.quote: Table 5 header and values: |Method|mIoU (%)|GFlops| ... |k = 1|79.2|**118.2**|
comments.explanation: Table 5 labels its third column as "GFlops", reporting values around 118-136. While dense prediction networks can be compute-heavy, the model is repeatedly marketed as a real-time architecture achieving ~200 FPS, which typically corresponds to a much lower compute footprint (often <50 GFLOPs on modern GPUs). Furthermore, Table 6 explicitly lists the parameter count for the baseline as ~0.76M. A sub-1M parameter model yielding 118 GFLOPs is possible but unusually high, strongly suggesting the column header may be a typo for "Parameters (M)", "MACs (B)", or "FLOPs (M)". This ambiguity regarding the exact computational unit could mislead readers regarding the true efficiency of the kernel size ablation.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses the important problem of real-time semantic segmentation but currently falls short of conference standards due to substantial concerns regarding technical clarity, novelty justification, and methodological completeness. The proposed architecture’s claim to operate without a traditional backbone is highly unconventional for modern segmentation tasks and is presented without architectural rationale, empirical validation, or clear explanation of how spatial hierarchy and semantic richness are preserved, raising serious questions about both validity and reproducibility. Clarity is further hampered by a generic, citation-heavy background and related work sections that catalog existing attention and context mechanisms without critically synthesizing how AASeg meaningfully advances beyond strong real-time baselines like BiSeNet, ESPNet, or ICNet. Additionally, the writing requires careful polishing to address grammatical inconsistencies, imprecise terminology, and underdeveloped explanations, while the methodological description remains structurally incomplete and lacks the rigorous mathematical formulation, implementation details, and ablation studies necessary to substantiate the claimed performance-speed tradeoff. To be competitive, the authors must fully articulate their architecture, rigorously defend the backbone-less design with controlled experiments, critically position their approach within the literature, and provide complete training/testing protocols alongside statistically sound comparisons.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel real-time semantic segmentation network (AASeg) that integrates spatial, channel, and multi-scale attention modules to balance accuracy and speed. While the motivation to address the speed-accuracy tradeoff is clear and the literature review covers standard baselines, the manuscript currently suffers from significant clarity issues and technical red flags regarding its feasibility. The mathematical notation in the proposed method is garbled (e.g., `$W[n][×][n]_$`), rendering the equations difficult to parse, and the writing style contains several grammatical errors. Most critically, the manuscript claims

comments.title: Missing Equation for Conv Block Output
comments.quote: we use ConvXi to denote the operations of i[th] block. Therefore, the output of i[th] block is calculated as follows: [blank line] Fusion operation is used to combine high-level features with low-level features using Equation 7:
comments.explanation: The text states that the output of the i-th block is calculated as follows but immediately transitions to describing the fusion operation without providing the definition or formula for Equation 9. The reader cannot determine how the features evolve within a single block before fusion.

comments.title: Undefined Tensor Product Operator in Aggregation
comments.quote: x_AASeg = (( fSA ( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat ) ⊕ ( fCA ( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat ) ⊕ ( fMSC ( xconcat ) ⊗ xconcat )) (5)
comments.explanation: The symbol '⊗' is used in Equation 5 to combine the attention outputs with the input features ($fSA(xconcat) ⊗ xconcat$). While context from Squeeze-and-Excitation networks suggests this represents element-wise multiplication, the paper neither defines the operator '⊗' nor clarifies that it differs from the previously defined '⊙' (convolution operator) in the background section. This ambiguity makes the mathematical formulation of the aggregation layer unclear.

comments.title: Missing Class Attention Loss Formula
comments.quote: The class attention loss $L_{cls}$ from channel attention module is also used. The class attention loss is [missing formula] where $a_{ijk}$ is the value generated of the class attention map of the $j[th]$ pixel...
comments.explanation: The authors introduce the 'class attention loss $L_{cls}$' to optimize the channel attention module but fail to provide the explicit formula for it. Instead, the text merely lists variable definitions ($a_{ijk}$) after the phrase 'The class attention loss is'. Without the loss function (e.g., a ranking loss or consistency loss), readers cannot reproduce the exact training objective for the attention mechanism.

comments.title: Major Architectural Inconsistency (2048 Channels vs No Backbone)
comments.quote: The output fused feature map is first convolved with a 1 × 1 convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The passage explicitly describes a network architecture that 'does not utilize a backbone' (stated in the Summary). However, the specific description of the Multi Scale Context (MSC) module references reducing channel counts 'from 2048', which is a characteristic output dimension of standard backbones (e.g., ResNet-101). Furthermore, this contradicts the reported low parameter counts (e.g., 0.76M in Table 6) and FLOPs (~80 GFLOPs in Table 3), as a network capable of processing 2048-channel feature maps without a backbone would typically require significantly more parameters and computation. This appears to be a corrupted text error.
